// RST_CTRL

    output                              ariane_rst_buf_noc2_ready_o,
    output [DataWidth-1:0]              ariane_rst_buf_noc3_data_o,
    output                              ariane_rst_buf_noc3_valid_o,

input  [DataWidth-1:0]              buf_ariane_rst_noc2_data_i,
    input                               buf_ariane_rst_noc2_valid_i,
    output                              ariane_rst_buf_noc2_ready_o,
    output [DataWidth-1:0]              ariane_rst_buf_noc3_data_o,
    output                              ariane_rst_buf_noc3_valid_o,

    output                              ariane_rst_buf_noc3_valid_o,
    input                               buf_ariane_rst_noc3_ready_i,

output                              ariane_rst_buf_noc2_ready_o,
    output [DataWidth-1:0]              ariane_rst_buf_noc3_data_o,
    output                              ariane_rst_buf_noc3_valid_o,
    input                               buf_ariane_rst_noc3_ready_i,

output [DataWidth-1:0]              ariane_rst_buf_noc3_data_o,

           .next(sha_new_msg),
           .block(sha_msg),
           .h_block(sha_h_block),

input  logic             tck_i,
    input  logic             trst_ni,

    input  dm::dmi_req_t     jtag_dmi_req_i,
    output logic             jtag_dmi_ready_o,
    input  logic             jtag_dmi_valid_i,

input  logic             clk_i,
    input  logic             rst_ni,

    output dm::dmi_req_t     core_dmi_req_o,
    output logic             core_dmi_valid_o,
    input  logic             core_dmi_ready_i,

cdc_2phase #(.T(dm::dmi_req_t)) i_cdc_req (
    .src_rst_ni  ( trst_ni          ),
    .src_clk_i   ( tck_i            ),
    .src_data_i  ( jtag_dmi_req_i   ),
    .src_valid_i ( jtag_dmi_valid_i ),
    .src_ready_o ( jtag_dmi_ready_o ),

.dst_rst_ni  ( rst_ni           ),
    .dst_clk_i   ( clk_i            ),
    .dst_data_o  ( core_dmi_req_o   ),
    .dst_valid_o ( core_dmi_valid_o ),
    .dst_ready_i ( core_dmi_ready_i )

cdc_2phase #(.T(dm::dmi_resp_t)) i_cdc_resp (
    .src_rst_ni  ( rst_ni           ),
    .src_clk_i   ( clk_i            ),
    .src_data_i  ( core_dmi_resp_i  ),
    .src_valid_i ( core_dmi_valid_i ),
    .src_ready_o ( core_dmi_ready_o ),

.dst_rst_ni  ( trst_ni          ),
    .dst_clk_i   ( tck_i            ),
    .dst_data_o  ( jtag_dmi_resp_o  ),
    .dst_valid_o ( jtag_dmi_valid_o ),
    .dst_ready_i ( jtag_dmi_ready_i )

input  logic         clk_i,      // DMI Clock
    input  logic         rst_ni,     // Asynchronous reset active low
    input  logic         testmode_i,
    input logic [`NUM_TILES*2-1:0]      priv_lvl_i,
    input  logic          we_flag,
    input  logic [255:0]  jtag_hash_i, ikey_hash_i, okey_hash_i,

output logic         jtag_unlock_o, 
    output logic         dmi_rst_no, // hard reset
    output dm::dmi_req_t dmi_req_o,
    output logic         dmi_req_valid_o,
    input  logic         dmi_req_ready_i,

if (test_logic_reset) begin
            dr_d = '0;
        end
    end

reset_facts.signal_or_condition: always_ff @(posedge tck_i or negedge trst_ni) begin

zero1        : '0,

                                dmistat      : dmi_error_i, // 0: No error, 1: Op failed, 2: too fast

csr_facts.text: enum logic [2:0] {Idle, WriteSecond, ReadSecond, WriteResp, ReadResp} state_d, state_q;
* FUSE mem: Which have all the secure data
* Description: Clock domain crossings for JTAG to DMI very heavily based
typedef enum logic [3:0] { Idle, Read, WaitReadValid, Write, WaitWriteValid, PassChk, PassChkWait, PassChkValid} state_e;
* Description: JTAG TAP for DMI (according to debug spec 0.13)

asset_candidates.label: jtag_hash / fuse-backed debug authentication reference
asset_candidates.reason: A 256-bit value from fuse memory is provided to the JTAG/DMI authentication path and compared/used by HMAC-related logic; candidate protected asset because it can authorize debug unlock.

asset_candidates.label: fuse memory read contents
asset_candidates.reason: Fuse memory is often security-sensitive; visible read request/address/data path makes the fuse array contents a protected asset candidate.

asset_candidates.label: JTAG unlock state
asset_candidates.reason: Unlock/pass state mediates debug/JTAG access and can change authorization for DMI operations.

asset_candidates.label: DMI request/response payload and debug module access
asset_candidates.reason: DMI transactions can read/write debug-module registers or control debug access; candidate protected control plane.

asset_candidates.label: core debug control outputs
asset_candidates.reason: Signals can request hart debug entry, reset non-debug module state, or activate debug functionality.

asset_candidates.label: debug-module bus master/slave channels
asset_candidates.reason: Debug module bus interfaces may access memory/peripherals and therefore are protected access paths/assets.

asset_candidates.label: peripheral access-control policy
asset_candidates.reason: Access-control and register-lock vectors appear to encode permission policy for multiple peripherals.

asset_candidates.label: crypto and security peripheral endpoints
asset_candidates.reason: Named crypto/RNG peripheral NoC request/response channels are likely to carry keys, plaintext/ciphertext, entropy, hashes, or control/status.

asset_candidates.label: HMAC key and intermediate digest state
asset_candidates.reason: HMAC key input and derived SHA/HMAC internal state are sensitive or authorization-relevant.

asset_candidates.label: boot ROM contents / boot selection
asset_candidates.reason: Boot ROM data and boot selection can affect trusted boot flow and software root of trust.

asset_candidates.label: interrupt/timer control state
asset_candidates.reason: CLINT/PLIC-related interrupt outputs and inputs may be privileged control-plane assets.

subject_candidates.label: external JTAG debugger
subject_candidates.reason: JTAG pins and TAP signals form an external debug requester path.

subject_candidates.label: JTAG TAP / DTM logic
subject_candidates.reason: TAP decodes JTAG instructions/data-register operations and issues DMI access controls.

subject_candidates.label: debug module / DMI endpoint
subject_candidates.reason: DMI endpoint receives debug requests and can act as a privileged requester toward core/debug resources.

subject_candidates.label: software or core privilege context
subject_candidates.reason: Privilege/debug-mode inputs identify CPU/software privilege state relevant to register or peripheral mediation.

subject_candidates.label: NoC/MMIO requesters targeting peripherals
subject_candidates.reason: Inbound NoC channels represent requesters that can access peripheral address spaces or registers.

subject_candidates.label: simulation debug/test requesters
subject_candidates.reason: Simulation/debug/test components and test mode may create alternate requester/control paths.

subject_candidates.label: DMA or fabric master
subject_candidates.reason: DMA channel is a non-CPU requester class commonly requiring permission mediation.

operation_candidates.label: DMI read/write operation
operation_candidates.reason: DMI op/address/data fields encode debug register transactions.

operation_candidates.label: JTAG scan/select/capture/update operation
operation_candidates.reason: TAP data-register operations shift data into/out of selected debug registers.

operation_candidates.label: debug unlock/authentication operation
operation_candidates.reason: HMAC/hash comparison flow appears to determine whether JTAG debug access is unlocked.

operation_candidates.label: fuse memory read operation
operation_candidates.reason: Request/address/data ports form a read path for fuse memory contents.

operation_candidates.label: peripheral access policy configuration or lock update
operation_candidates.reason: Access-control and register-lock control signals plus write-enable flags suggest policy/lock configuration operations.

operation_candidates.label: debug reset/activation operation
operation_candidates.reason: Debug and JTAG reset/active signals control security-sensitive operating state.

operation_candidates.label: NoC peripheral read/write transactions
operation_candidates.reason: Valid/ready/data channels to peripherals represent software/fabric-visible operations.

operation_candidates.label: HMAC compute/hash bypass operation
operation_candidates.reason: HMAC module can compute authentication hash and includes a key-hash bypass control input.

guard_candidates.label: JTAG HMAC/hash authentication guard
guard_candidates.expected_guard_role: Authenticate or authorize external JTAG requester before permitting protected debug operations.

guard_candidates.label: privilege-level mediation
guard_candidates.expected_guard_role: Use CPU privilege/debug mode to restrict access to privileged control/status/peripheral resources.

guard_candidates.label: register lock / write-protect guard
guard_candidates.expected_guard_role: Prevent modification of locked configuration or protected registers after lock assertion.

guard_candidates.label: peripheral access-control matrix guard
guard_candidates.expected_guard_role: Allow or deny requesters/privilege classes per peripheral.

guard_candidates.label: DMI valid/ready and busy/error mediation
guard_candidates.expected_guard_role: Ensure requests are accepted only in permitted/ready states and error/busy state is reflected.

guard_candidates.label: JTAG TAP instruction/data-register select guard
protects_or_mediates: which register is shifted to TDO
guard_candidates.expected_guard_role: Decode/select the intended JTAG register and prevent unintended exposure or access.

guard_candidates.label: test mode / DFT clock selection guard
guard_candidates.expected_guard_role: Constrain test-mode paths so they do not bypass normal debug authorization.

guard_candidates.label: reset guards and reset-domain separation
guard_candidates.expected_guard_role: Reset sensitive states to safe values and avoid stale authorization/residue across reset domains.

state_lifecycle_facts.label: HMAC authentication state machine lifecycle
state_lifecycle_facts.permission_relevance: Authentication result and derived hash state can control debug unlock decisions.

state_lifecycle_facts.label: JTAG unlock/pass lifecycle
state_lifecycle_facts.permission_relevance: Unlock state determines when JTAG/DMI access should be permitted.

state_lifecycle_facts.label: DMI request/response handshake lifecycle
state_lifecycle_facts.permission_relevance: Authorization and request acceptance need to remain coherent across handshakes and CDC.

state_lifecycle_facts.label: TAP reset and scan lifecycle
state_lifecycle_facts.permission_relevance: TAP state changes define when data is loaded, shifted out, or committed to DMI operations.

state_lifecycle_facts.label: fuse address/data read lifecycle
state_lifecycle_facts.permission_relevance: Fuse data may be exposed or used as an authentication reference depending on read path mediation.

state_lifecycle_facts.label: register-lock/access-control lifecycle
state_lifecycle_facts.permission_relevance: Policy and lock state should mediate all relevant write/access paths after configuration.

state_lifecycle_facts.label: debug reset/activation lifecycle
state_lifecycle_facts.permission_relevance: Debug control can change CPU execution and reset state; should be guarded by authorization.

state_lifecycle_facts.label: testmode / DFT clock path lifecycle
state_lifecycle_facts.permission_relevance: Test-mode transitions can alter JTAG/TAP behavior and may need lifecycle authorization.

path_candidates.label: external JTAG pins to DMI request path
fuse memory to JTAG authentication path
JTAG password/challenge data to HMAC result path
NoC/MMIO requester to security peripheral channels
software/core privilege to peripheral access policy
debug module bus master path toward system fabric
subjects: external JTAG debugger after DMI mediation

path_candidates.label: JTAG TAP output exposure path
guards_to_check: unlock/auth state if DMI data is selected

path_candidates.label: debug reset path to core/platform control
fuse readout to software-visible data path

summary: Permission-oriented semantic labeling for visible RTL scope: debug/JTAG DMI path, fuse-backed JTAG hash material, HMAC authentication logic, peripheral access-control and register-lock signals, NoC/MMIO-style peripheral channels, reset/debug control outputs, and DFT/test-mode JTAG TAP controls are the main security-relevant areas. Labels are candidates only, not vulnerability conclusions.
